sections.label: Let us do these activities before we read

questions.question_html:
Answer the following questions and share them with your teacher and classmates.

  1. Have you ever flown a kite or seen someone fly it? Where? When?
  2. Describe the kite-colour(s), shape, design of tail, any other detail.
  3. What did you think when you saw the kite up in the sky?
questions.solution_html:
1. Yes, I have flown a kite. I did this during the Makar Sankranti festival, which is a popular kite-flying event in India. It took place on the terrace of my house in January when the weather was clear and windy, perfect for kite flying.
  2. The kite I flew was vibrant and colourful. It had a diamond shape with bright red, yellow, and blue patterns. The tail was made of long, multi-coloured strips of paper that fluttered gracefully in the wind. The frame was lightweight, made from bamboo sticks, and the kite itself was crafted from thin, durable paper.
  3. When I saw the kite up in the sky, I felt a sense of joy and freedom. It was mesmerizing to watch it soar higher and higher, dancing with the wind. The sight of the colourful kite against the blue sky was captivating, and it made me feel connected to a tradition that has been enjoyed for generations.

questions.question_html:
Search the internet for videos on Kite Festivals and watch them with your teacher and classmates.

  1. Share the things with your teacher that you saw and what the people were doing.
  2. Would you like to participate in this kind of festival? Why?
  3. What kind of kite would you like to fly?
questions.solution_html:
1. This is an activity. So do yourself under the guidance of elder or teachers.
  2. Yes, I would love to participate in this kind of festival. Kite flying is not only a fun activity but also a way to connect with friends, family, and the community. The excitement of seeing kites of different shapes and colours fill the sky is exhilarating. Additionally, participating in such a festival allows me to experience cultural traditions, enjoy the outdoors, and create wonderful memories.
  3. I would like to fly a dragon-shaped kite. It would be large, with vibrant green and gold colours, and designed to look like a mythical dragon. The tail would be long and adorned with intricate patterns and streamers to mimic the dragon's body. This kite would be eye-catching and unique, making it stand out among the others in the sky.

questions.question_html:
Look at the picture and complete the sentences. Share your responses with your teacher.

windy, kite, flying, birds, sky, looking, has ribbons
  1. I can see ________, ________ and ________ in the picture.
  2. The weather in the picture is ________.
  3. The kite is ________ high in the sky.
  4. The child is ________ from below.
  5. The tail of the kite ________.
questions.solution_html:
1. kite, birds, a child
  2. windly
  3. flying
  4. looking up
  5. has ribbons

questions.question_html:
Now, think and answer.

  1. List two more things that you want to add to the picture.
  2. If I were the child, I would ________.
  3. If I were the kite, I would ________.
questions.solution_html:
1. List two more things that you want to add to the picture.
    • A colourful picnic blanket with some snacks and a basket nearby.
    • A group of friends or family members also enjoying kite flying and having fun.
  2. If I were the child, I would run around joyfully, trying to make the kite fly higher and higher, while laughing and feeling the wind on my face.
  3. If I were the kite, I would soar gracefully in the sky, enjoying the view from above and dancing with the wind, feeling free and adventurous.

questions.question_html:
Read the poem silently. As you read, mark the given statements as True or False.

Statements T/F
(a) The child is looking at the kites.  
(b) The kites look like birds of different colours.  
(c) It was a rainy day.  
(d) The child wants to be like a kite.  
(e) The child wishes to climb on a kite.  
(f) The kite is made of cloth and plastic.  
(g) The child wants to ride the kite.  
(h) The child knows they would have fun.  
(i) The child wants to look at the kite from a rooftop.  
(j) The child would like to look at people down below.  
(k) The child knows that the people would stare.
questions.solution_html:
Statements T/F
(a) The child is looking at the kites. True
(b) The kites look like birds of different colours. True
(c) It was a rainy day. False
(d) The child wants to be like a kite. True
(e) The child wishes to climb on a kite. True
(f) The kite is made of cloth and plastic. False
(g) The child wants to ride the kite. True
(h) The child knows they would have fun. True
(i) The child wants to look at the kite from a rooftop. False
(j) The child would like to look at people down below. True
(k) The child knows that the people would stare. True

questions.question_html:
Complete the following sentences.

  1. The poet says that the kites are like coloured birds – See the kites fly/Like coloured birds in the sky. The kites have been compared to b _ _ _ _ because they ________.
  2. The child wishes to be like air - I wish I were small / And as light as air.
    The child wishes to be as l _ _ _ t as __ __ __. The child wishes this to be able to c _ _ m _ on a kite and fly.

The poet uses simile in (a) and (b) to compare the kites with coloured birds and the quality of being light as air. The poet uses 'like' and 'as' to compare.
Do you think that the use of simile in this poem helps us imagine better when we read the poem? Share your thoughts with your teacher and classmates.
questions.solution_html:
1. The poet says that the kites are like coloured birds -
    "See the kites fly / Like coloured birds in the sky." The kites have been compared to birds because they fly gracefully and have vibrant colours.
  2. The child wishes to be like air - "I wish I were small / And as light as air."
    The child wishes to be as light as air. The child wishes this to be able to climb on a kite and fly.

Thoughts on the Use of Simile in the Poem
The use of simile in this poem indeed helps us imagine better when we read it. By comparing the kites to "coloured birds," the poet paints a vivid picture of the kites' movement and beauty, making it easier for readers to visualize them soaring in the sky. Similarly, the comparison of the child's desired lightness to that of air helps convey the sense of freedom and weightlessness the child yearns for. These similes enhance the imagery and emotional connection, allowing readers to feel the child's longing and the beauty of the scene more deeply.

questions.question_html:
Find a set of words from the poem that begin with the same consonant sound.
Stanza 1
Which consonant sound do both words begin with?
Stanza 3
Which consonant sound do both words begin with?
This is called alliteration. For example, big bright blue bag, funny fan, etc.
Create 4 other sets of words using alliteration.
questions.solution_html:
Stanza 1
"wind-whipped"
Consonant sound: w
Stanza 3
"paper wings"
Consonant sound: p
Examples of Alliteration
Here are four other sets of words using alliteration:

  • Sunny skies
  • Bouncing balls
  • Tiny turtles
  • Clever cats

questions.question_html: In the last stanza, the two words that the poet repeats are ________, ________ to tell us that the kite is flying _ _ _ y high.
questions.solution_html: In the last stanza, the two words that the poet repeats are high, high to tell us that the kite is flying very high.

questions.question_html: Study each stanza and underline the rhyming words from the end of each line. Also, circle the end words in the stanzas that do not rhyme.
questions.solution_html:
Stanza 1
Underlined rhyming words:

  • fly
  • sky

Stanza 2
Underlined rhyming words:

  • air
  • there

Stanza 3
Underlined rhyming words:

  • wings
  • sings

Stanza 4
Underlined rhyming words:

  • down
  • town

Stanza 5
Underlined rhyming words:

  • stare
  • air

Rhyming Words in Each Stanza:

  • fly and sky
  • air and there
  • wings and sings
  • down and town
  • stare and air

questions.question_html:
Read the given lines from the poem and answer the following questions.
What fun it would be
To look right down,
Over the park
And the rooftops of town.

  1. Circle the word that does not share the same feeling as ‘fun’:
    joy, excitement, care, happiness.
  2. Which line tells us that the poet was somewhere above?
  3. Choose the scene the child saw.
questions.solution_html:
1. The word that does not share the same feeling as 'fun' is care.
    joy, excitement, , happiness.
  2. "To look right down,"
    This line indicates that the poet imagines being high above, looking down over the park and rooftops of the town.
  3. Based on the poem, the child is imagining looking down from high up in the air, seeing the park and the rooftops of the town.
    The appropriate scene that matches this description is:
    Scene (ii): This scene shows an aerial view of houses and trees, which aligns with the child's perspective of looking down over the park and rooftops of the town from a high vantage point.

questions.question_html: How does the poet describe the kites in the first two lines? Answer in the context of the chapter The Kites.
questions.solution_html: The poet describes the kites as flying up in the air like coloured birds in a wind-whipped sky.

questions.question_html: Why does the child say that they have to climb a tree first to get onto the kite?
questions.solution_html: The poem doesn't explicitly mention climbing a tree to get onto the kite, but the child wishes to be light as air to climb on a kite. If climbing a tree were part of their imaginative scenario, it might be because they perceive a tree as a means to get closer to the kite, bridging the gap between the ground and the sky.

questions.question_html: What are the songs that the child hears when flying atop the kite? Answer in the context of the chapter The Kites.
questions.solution_html: The child imagines hearing the songs that the wild wind sings, which can be interpreted as the various sounds and melodies created by the wind.

questions.question_html: Why did the people look up at the child and stare? If you looked up and saw that, what feelings would you have? Answer in the context of the chapter The Kites.
questions.solution_html: The people looked up and stared because seeing a child riding a kite high in the air is an unusual and astonishing sight. If I looked up and saw that, I would likely feel a mix of amazement, wonder, and perhaps a bit of concern for the child's safety.

questions.question_html: If you were the kite carrying the child high up in the air, what would you tell them? Answer in the context of the chapter The Kites.
questions.solution_html: If I were the kite carrying the child high up in the air, I would tell them to enjoy the view and the freedom of soaring above the world, to hold on tight and feel the wind rushing past, and to take in the beauty and wonder of seeing everything from such a unique perspective. I would also reassure them that they are safe and to cherish this magical experience.

questions.question_html:
Look at the picture of the kite and read what some of its parts are named.

Now, complete the given conversation by filling the blanks with some ‘parts of a kite’ words from above.
Bina: I love the long yellow t _ _ _ at the end of my kite.
What colour is your kite?
Saroj: My kite’s c _ _ _ _ is pink. The s _ _ _ _ e is black.
Bina: Oh wonderful! My kite l _ _ _ is red. The r_ _ l that wraps it is green. I really like it.
Saroj: My kite line is red too. The tip of my kite has a blue _ _ _ e.
Bina: Beautiful! Let’s go and fly our pretty kite.
questions.solution_html:
Bina: I love the long yellow tail at the end of my kite. What colour is your kite?
Saroj: My kite's cover is pink. The spine is black.
Bina: Oh wonderful! My kite line is red. The reel that wraps it is green. I really like it.
Saroj: My kite line is red too. The tip of my kite has a blue nose.
Bina: Beautiful! Let's go and fly our pretty kite.

questions.question_html:
Let us create a poem using words in a kite!

The first line is the subject. Keep this to one word. Use a noun. kite
The second line is two describing words for the noun. pretty, colourful
Line three includes three action words. flying, floating, soaring
Line four uses two words that describe the action words. quickly, happily
Line five is one word that has the same meaning as the subject/synonym. paper-bird


This is called a cinquain poem. It is a five line poem that describes a person, a place or a thing.
Now, create a cinquain poem yourself on anything you like.

The poet uses the word fly and drift to tell us about the kite’s movement.

  1. Circle the words that match with the ‘drift’ movement. You can choose more than one word.
    slow, fast, quick, upward, downward, round and round, float
  2. Study some other words about the kite’s movement and share what type of movement they show. You can look at (a) for the type of movement.
    glide, dive, soar, swoop, circle
questions.solution_html:
1. The words that match with the 'drift' movement:
    , fast, quick, upward, , round and round, float
  2. Other words about the kite's movement and type of movement they show:
    • Glide: A smooth and continuous movement, often slow and graceful, similar to drifting.
    • Dive: A fast and downward movement, often sudden.
    • Soar: A fast, upward movement, often high and with sustained motion.
    • Swoop: A fast movement, typically downward and then upward again, often sudden.
    • Circle: A movement going round and round, maintaining a steady path.

questions.question_html:
The poet has used expressions like-
up in the air; look right down; stay up there; stand and stare

  1. Read how these expressions are used in the poem.
  2. In pairs, make sentences using these expressions. Discuss with your teacher and then write in your notebooks.
questions.solution_html:
1. The expressions used in the poem convey different actions and positions related to the kite and the child's imagination. Here's how they are used in the poem:
    up in the air: Refers to the kites flying high above the ground.
    look right down: Describes the child's imagined perspective of looking down from high up in the air.
    stay up there: The desire to remain in the high position, enjoying the view and experience.
    stand and stare: Describes the reaction of people on the ground, looking up in amazement.
  2. Here are sentences using these expressions:
    Up in the air: The hot air balloons floated gracefully up in the air, creating a colorful spectacle against the morning sky.
    Look right down: From the top of the Ferris wheel, you can look right down and see the entire amusement park.
    Stay up there: The bird perched on the branch seemed to want to stay up there all day, enjoying the cool breeze.
    Stand and stare: The fireworks display was so mesmerizing that the entire crowd could only stand and stare in awe.

questions.question_html:
Listen about the Kite Festival in India. As you listen, circle the words that are not correct. Then, listen again and write the correct words. (refer to page 163 for transcript)

  1. Kite Festival is celebrated during the month of June.
  2. The International Kite Festival is named Uttarayan in Eastern India.
  3. Uttarayan is mainly celebrated in Gujarat, but also in Maharashtra and Telangana.
  4. During the Kite Festival, we can see kites of different shapes, sizes and colours.
  5. In Karnataka, the Tourism Department organises the kite festival every year.
  6. The Punjab region celebrates Basant Panchami and Baisakhi by flying kites.
  7. People of India also enjoy flying kites on Earth Day.
questions.solution_html:
1. Kite Festival is celebrated during the month of June.
    Incorrect: June
    Correct: January
  2. The International Kite Festival is named Uttarayan in Eastern India.
    Incorrect: Eastern
    Correct: Western
  3. Uttarayan is mainly celebrated in Gujarat, but also in Maharashtra and Telangana.
    Incorrect: Maharashtra
    Correct: Rajasthan
  4. During the Kite Festival, we can see kites of different shapes, sizes and colours.
    This sentence is correct.
  5. In Karnataka, the Tourism Department organises the kite festival every year.
    This sentence is correct.
  6. The Punjab region celebrates Basant Panchami and Baisakhi by flying kites.
    This sentence is correct.
  7. People of India also enjoy flying kites on Earth Day.
    Incorrect: Earth Day
    Correct: Independence Day

questions.question_html: Share a kite-flying experience with your classmates. If you have not flown a kite yet, share an experience of watching kites fly. Give details- Where? When? Why? How? Speak in complete sentences.
questions.solution_html:
My Kite-Flying Experience

One of my most memorable kite-flying experiences took place a few years ago during Makar Sankranti in January. This festival is widely celebrated in India, and it's a time when the sky is filled with colorful kites.
Where
I was at my grandparents' house in a small town in Gujarat. Their house has a large terrace, which is perfect for flying kites.
When
We started early in the morning, just after breakfast. The entire day was dedicated to kite flying, as this is a significant part of the Makar Sankranti celebrations.
Why
Makar Sankranti is a festival that marks the transition of the sun into the zodiac sign of Capricorn. It is celebrated with much enthusiasm, and kite flying is a major tradition associated with this festival. My family and I participated to enjoy the festive spirit and spend quality time together.
How
We prepared for the day by buying several kites and rolls of string the previous day. On the day of the festival, we gathered on the terrace. My cousins and I were taught how to properly handle the kites and the strings. With the help of my uncle, I managed to get my kite up in the air. It was a beautiful yellow kite with a long tail that danced in the wind. As the day progressed, we engaged in friendly kite-flying competitions with our neighbours. The sky was dotted with kites of all shapes, sizes, and colours, creating a mesmerizing view.
Overall, it was an exhilarating experience. The joy of seeing my kite soar high and the thrill of trying to outmanoeuvre other kites in the sky made it an unforgettable day. Even though my hands were a bit sore from handling the string, the excitement and happiness of the day were worth it.

questions.question_html:
Imagine yourself as a kite. In pairs, first discuss all the following hints.

  • What shape of kite are you? What colours do you have? What other parts of yourself are you happy about?
  • Where are you? Who do you belong to? What activities do you do? Who are your friends?
  • Do you have any message for humans?
questions.solution_html:
Shape, Colours, and Parts
Shape: I am a diamond-shaped kite.
Colours: I have vibrant rainbow colours with stripes of red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet.
Parts: I am proud of my long, flowing tail decorated with small, colourful bows. My spine is strong, and my cover is made of a light but durable material that catches the wind perfectly.
Location, Belonging, and Activities
Where are you? I am soaring high above a vast, open field, filled with children and families enjoying a beautiful sunny day.
Who do you belong to? I belong to a cheerful young boy who loves to fly me every chance he gets. His excitement and joy when I soar high make me feel special.
Activities: I love gliding, swooping, and performing loops in the sky. My favourite activity is catching a strong breeze that takes me higher and higher.
Friends: My friends are other kites of various shapes and sizes. We sometimes engage in friendly aerial dances, showcasing our colours and manoeuvres.
Message for Humans
My message for humans is simple: take time to look up and appreciate the beauty of the sky. Remember the joy and freedom of being a child. Let go of your worries for a moment and find happiness in simple pleasures, like flying a kite. Enjoy nature, cherish your loved ones, and always keep your spirit light and free.

questions.question_html: Now, write a paragraph of about 100 words, with the title- I am a Kite.
questions.solution_html:
I am a Kite

I am a vibrant, diamond-shaped kite adorned with the colours of the rainbow, proudly displaying stripes of red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet. My long, flowing tail, decorated with colourful bows, dances gracefully in the wind. I belong to a joyful young boy who eagerly flies me high above an open field on sunny days. Together, we soar, glide, and swoop, enjoying the freedom of the sky. My friends are other kites of various shapes and sizes, and we often perform aerial dances, showcasing our beauty. As I float high above, I remind humans to cherish simple pleasures, embrace nature, and keep their spirits light and free.

questions.question_html: The mention of kites exists in ancient Indian texts. It can be found in the poetry of the thirteenth-century Marathi saint and poet, Namadeva. In his poems or gathas, he called a kite a gudi, and there is a mention that the kites were made from kaagad (paper).
questions.solution_html: The Ancient Tradition of Kites in India: The tradition of kite flying in India is ancient, with mentions in historical texts and poetry. In the thirteenth century, the Marathi saint and poet Namadeva referred to kites as gudi in his gathas (poems). He described kites being made from kaagad, meaning paper. This indicates that kite flying was a popular and cherished activity even in ancient times. Namadeva's poetic references highlight the cultural significance and the joy associated with kite flying in Indian society. This tradition has been passed down through generations, continuing to bring delight and excitement to people of all ages across India.

questions.question_html:
Have a look at some of the different types of kites. Choose which one is commonly seen.

  1. Find out the steps to make a kite.
  2. Follow the steps and create your own kite. Get it to school to show your teacher and classmates.
  3. Write a message on the kite. For example: Happy Independance Day!
questions.solution_html:
Among the different types of kites shown in the image, the Diamond-shaped kite is the most commonly seen.
Steps to Make a Diamond-shaped Kite
Materials Needed:

  • Two wooden dowels (one longer for the spine, one shorter for the crossbar)
  • A large piece of lightweight paper or fabric (kite cover)
  • String or strong thread
  • Glue or tape
  • Scissors
  • A ruler
  • Markers or paint for decoration (optional)
  • A long piece of lightweight ribbon or fabric for the tail

Instructions:
Create the Frame:

  • Measure and cut the dowels. The longer dowel (spine) should be about 24 inches, and the shorter dowel (crossbar) about 20 inches.
  • Lay the shorter dowel across the longer one to form a cross, about one-third of the way down the longer dowel.
  • Secure the two dowels together using string or strong tape at the intersection. Make sure they form a right angle.

Make the Cover:

  • Place the frame on the paper or fabric and trace the outline of the kite, leaving an extra inch around the edges for folding.
  • Cut out the kite shape from the paper or fabric.
  • Fold the edges of the paper or fabric over the frame and secure with glue or tape.

Attach the String:

  • Cut a piece of string about twice the length of the spine.
  • Tie one end of the string to the top of the spine and the other end to the bottom.
  • Attach another piece of string at the intersection of the dowels for the kite line.

Add the Tail:

  • Attach the ribbon or fabric to the bottom of the kite. This helps to stabilize the kite in the wind.

Decorate:

  • Use markers or paint to decorate your kite. You can write a message like "Happy Independence Day!" on it.

Creating and Flying Your Kite

  • Follow the steps above to create your own kite.
  • Once your kite is ready, take it to an open area with plenty of wind.
  • Fly your kite and enjoy!
